speaker: interviewer
text: It is February 25, 1996. We're doing a Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation interview with Martin Koby, K-O-B-Y. Interviewer is Geoff Reynolds, Videographer is Bill Harter. We're in Southfield, Michigan, USA. The language spoken is English. Survivor mic channel one, interviewer mic channel two. My name is Geoffrey Reynolds. It's February 25, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Martin Koby, in Southfield, Michigan, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Martin Koby. I live at 27319 Fairfax, Southfield, Michigan, 48076.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth, Martin?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name? At birth, I was named Moshe ben Anshel. My father's-- that's my patronymic name. And I lived as Kobylanski until I was 31 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell those?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Hebrew name to start with?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-S-H-E, transliterated, and ben, 'son of,' my father's name, Anshel, A-N-S-H-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you spell your last?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-B-Y-L-A-N-S-K-I. Sometimes it was spelled with a K-I-J on the end and sometimes K-I-Y. Why it was that way, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you get your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born November 25, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Current age is 65. I just celebrated my 65th birthday last November.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city you currently live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in Southfield, Michigan, S-O-U-T-H-F-I-E-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michigan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you name and spell the city where you were born and the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, in 1930. And I was born-- born in the city of Rowne, R-O-V-N-O. But since infancy, I lived in this village of Hruszwica, G-R-U-S-Z-W-I-C-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Bill. Martin, can you tell me about life in Poland before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] As a kid, as I remember, it was kind of pleasant, very nice. I had friends. I had my relatives in our village. Most of my relatives in our village were on my mother's side, because my mother was a native of the village of Hruszwica. Naturally, there were cousins and uncles and aunts and my grandma. And there were other-- there was another bigger Jewish family. And we were always friends. We-- I used to go visit my cousins or visit the Adlers, the boys. We used to play. We also had Hebrew classes together, mostly in the summer. Where everybody had vacation, we attended Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's like Sunday school. Because our parents could not afford to get a teacher during the year, therefore, summer was a combined intensive education.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had also a-- two cousins on my father's side. One of my uncles, Uncle Chaim, lived in our village also. And he was married to one of my mother's cousins. Unfortunately, I never knew that she was a cousin until somewhere in the 1960s. Upon a discussion-- during a-- during a discussion with my mother, I found out that, first, she was my-- she was like my second cousin. And then, she turns out to be my aunt. And I always thought she was my aunt. So her children were my cousins, I considered as my cousins. And I used to visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your mother and father's name and any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name, as I said before, was Anshel. Here, in the United States, he became Albert Kobylanski. My mother's name was Raizel. And in the United States, she became Rose. My father was a native in the region of Sarna, S-A-R-N-A, which is like northwest part of our region. And I think about it in terms of Appalachia, poor, not mountainous, but rivers and sand. And they had a very hard time making a living. My father, when he married my mother, they lived in Rowne for I don't know how long. I assume, maybe, two or three or four years. I really never found out. But we ended up living in Hruszwica, because it was my mother's native village. And that's where they settled. That's where we lived. As far as I remember, school was-- just, you know, life was, to a kid, it was very pleasant. There were nice summers, the river and the meadows and the hills, picking somebody else's cherries or looking for apples. We did not have an orchard, because we had very-- very little land. Going visiting my cousins. Playing in-- in barns. Going for hikes through the forest. Swimming in the river. Fishing, you know. We did not have any fishing equipment. Mostly, it was done with bushel basket, which were woven out of-- made out of willow tree branches. Dam up the river and catch the fish on the other side. Catch-- we had very many frogs during the spring, lots of frogs. That was fun to catch. And then, there was Hebrew school every day during the summer. It was very hard. Everybody-- my friends, the Ukrainian friends, used to come and call me, taunt me, you know, to come and play while I had to spend, here, my time with the teacher. But I'm very glad that my parents insisted that I learned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you went to school with any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have a brother, a younger brother. Yitzhak is-- over there, he was Yitzhak. In fact, one of the babysitters still calls him Yitzhak, you know, Isaac. But he became a Robert. Our-- when we came to the United States, in 1947, we all were given new names, anglicized. [LAUGHS] And my brother's about five years younger. He was-- to me, he was always a pain, because I had to babysit with him. I couldn't go and play with my friends. So he's always a problem. But somehow, we grew up. And we're getting along just fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as school? School over there, I went to school every day during school season, so September to end of June. We used to go to school six days a week, Monday through Saturday, because it was a Christian school, a public school. But most of the Saturdays, I think my parents arranged that I could skip school on Saturdays. It was very nice. But I had to go to-- used to always go with my father to the synagogue for religious prayers. Looking back now, that was very nice. I used to be very proud.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was Jewish life like in your city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our village? It was just quiet. We were all still-- our parents were observant. Naturally, we had to follow their footsteps. We did not work on Saturday. We did not start fires on Saturday. We went visiting, eating. And on Saturday, my father used to take a nap. He used to manage to take a nap all the time, especially on Saturday, which my mother did not look with-- up with favor. But he used to give me the prayer book and made me practice. And I had to pronounce it loud, so he could hear me. And I used to think that he didn't hear, that he was really sleeping. But he heard every word. But nowadays, it comes in very handy. He told me [MOAN] I want you to learn. [MOAN] I want you to know it, that when you come amongst your people, you should not be ashamed that you don't know [SOB] how to. [SOBBING] And I'm glad he instilled it into me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] Otherwise, I used to go and play with the Adler boys. We used to go-- also, they had a cousin, which was not related to me. We used to go to his house. We used to play over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was always something to do. Especially in the summer, if it rained in the morning, we were able to go swimming in a mud hole, in one of those-- a little puddle. But you know, when you're 6, 7, 8, so, what's the difference? Water is water, even if it's dirty. How did the non-Jewish people treat you in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was a-- it was a dual treatment. Sometimes, you were very nice. You were accepted. You were respected, as far as observers of eating non-kosher foods. You were one of the boys. You were one of the group. And sometimes, they used to turn on you, right there and then. Here they were nice, and, all of a sudden, it was-- everything turned topsy-turvy. And you're being a one and them more, you-- you could not defend yourself very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time, it was the-- a religious-- the religious slurs and religious accusations. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And not being, you know, yet old enough to understand all of this, I used to cry, used to run away, and come home and tell the parents. You know, why? I ask questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was difficult to understand why that was happening. Some of my friends used to-- I remember, one time, my brother's babysitter, with whom I- she used to be a neighbor, next door neighbor. And I think I'll never forget it. The baby-- the lady-- the girl babysitter, she must have been older than I was. She brought-- there were some of her friends were over at her house. And since we lived next door, well, you walk out the door, here you see the next door, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, somehow, they invited-- lured me into their house to the-- to the-- or to the-- onto their backyard. Naturally, with all these ladies, and who doesn't want to have friends and play with? So I went over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they wanted to see-- to see me, to-- Olga, the next door neighbor, told her like friends that she-- that they'll be able to see-- to see a Jewish boy circumcised. Well, I wasn't going to volunteer, naturally. And the-- they wrestled me down to the ground. They pulled down my pants. And they saw a Jewish boy circumcised. Well, since 1991, '92, I started to correspond with Olga again-- or for the first time. Because it's the only person I could remember the name and the address. And I reminded her, so, I-- I remember, once upon a time, you caught me. And, you know, wrestled me down to the ground and pulled my pants down so you could show off what a-- well, what a circumcision looks like. I said, another time, I remember, you took pork, and you smeared my lips on it. You-- you wanted me to eat it. But I wouldn't. So you rubbed the pork against my lips. And you made me think that-- think of myself, that I've committed a sin.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, I survived. Here I am writing to you. Do you remember that? She never answered. She says, yeah, I remember some things. But she did not acknowledge, you know, that she did that. But it's OK. No problem. How did your teachers teach you? Or how did they treat you in the Christian school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were teachers who were very nice. There was no problem with some teacher. With some teachers, it was horrendous. One year we had a teacher. His name mister-- was Mr. Dabrowski. And the-- he was not very nice to me. He always found an-- an excuse to punish me. And also, he tried to force me to stand on the front of the class and tell them stories that I felt were not right, were not fair. One particular incident, he-- I don't know when exactly. But I do remember, it was in the spring, because he had a-- a whip made out of a willow tree. And you know, the new sprout, they're very straight, new branches. Cut it to maybe three feet long. And he was holding it on his desk. And I went up to-- he called me up to the front of the class, to face the class. He said, and OK, Moishe, Moshe, tell the class how Jews make candy out of coal in order to-- and-- and they sell it to Christian children to poison them. Well, even at that age, it did not sit well with me, because I always was under the impression that candy are made out of sugar or sugar beet syrup or some sort. Well, I would not. I just defied him. I said I would not tell him. I told the teacher, I cannot say this, because that's not true. Well, he didn't like it, because I said it in front of the whole class, which is defying his authority. I know it now, but I think I-- I understood it then, too. Well, he punished me. First, I had to show my palms of my hands. And he took that-- that willow stick and let you have it over the hand. So he got in two hits. But the-- the next couple, you know, I withdrew my hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had some kind of a blouse. He knocked every button off my blouse that I-- of my shirt. Well, which upset him, because I was-- they were going to ask me at home, what happened to your buttons? So I had to tell them that the teacher did it. So for not obeying his orders to-- to have my hands ready for-- for the whipping, he put me in the corner of the class and put dried beans and peas on the floor. And I had to kneel on that.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's very hard to stand on. But you find a way, you know, to get the beans and the peas from under your knees, to-- you know, to-- to get away from that painful situation. But he made sure that there was always those peas and beans under my knees. Other teachers never did that. We had-- had some very nice teachers. I still remember learning the alphabet and multiplication and spelling and geography. It was very nice, except for this particular teacher, who was terrible. I used to enjoy Fridays, the last hour of Friday, before we were dismissed. On that day, the-- the class used to be-- they receive religious instructions. The Catholics went with a Catholic priest. And the Ukrainian were Russian Orthodox. That was the-- the Russian church. And they received the-- there used to come the priest to teach them. Well, I was always excluded from the Catholic service in our classes. Never allowed to go there. But in the Russian, if I choose, I could stay. If I choose, I could go home. My cousin, Pinchas, that lives now in-- in Israel, he always left. He would go home, because he just had a very good excuse not to stay in school. Dummy me, I stayed. I wanted to see what it's like, what they're being taught or what a-- how the class is conducted and what's being taught in a Christian-- Christian religion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I was very much surprised. My children went to school here, to Hebrew school here. They always used to come home. And they said-- complain, there's chaos in our Hebrew classes. Why do we have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I used to question them. No one pays attention. The teacher cares-- cannot exert discipline. There's always screaming and hollering. That's exactly what was in the Russian religious class. The priest-- the priest was dressed formally, in his priest outfit, with a big staff, you know, like you see in pictures, Moses walking with a staff or a prophet walking, you know, having a big staff with a crook on top. And that priest, that-- the Russian priest came dressed with a tall, black hat, a nice robe, and with this staff. So he used the staff-- well, we used to-- in that particular class, we used to sit on a bench, you know, about five or six boys together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So naturally, he couldn't run around the whole class just to come to punish the kid that sat on the end of the bench. The bench must have been about five, six, maybe seven feet long.

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to use that rod, the staff, to hit the kid on the-- on over there. It was very nice. It was. But I didn't go every-- every Friday. Some Fridays I went. If I knew there were chores to do at home, I stayed for [LAUGHS] for the religious instructions in the Russian church. But the-- the-- the essence of the instruction was usually selected from the Old Testament. So I felt very comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your village laid out in conjunction with Christians and Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- there were eight Jewish families in our village. So the founding fathers, let's say, that founded the-- my mother's family had a-- they had a house. They had a nice estate in-- in those-- at-- at that time and those-- in that situation. And next door, there was the other Jewish founding family. So naturally, when they had children, everybody married. Most of them married. You know, they established their own household. So all in all, there were eight Jewish families. I would say half were living in the southern part, southwestern part, which was Czech. And the rest, the other half, lived among the Ukrainian, scattered. The-- the-- there only were two Jewish families lived next door to each other. The rest of them were farther apart. Our village was laid out-- I finally, after all these years, figured out how it was laid out. First of all, right through the middle of the village was a river, a little-- little river, not. In the summer, sometimes, it used to dry up. But most of the time, it was water in there, but sort of a narrow place. And on both sides were meadows, very nice meadows. We used to have fun there. And naturally, after the meadows, the elevation changed. And all the way around that river that was part of the village. But I-- finally dawned on me, lately, that the village was laid out, like, if you took two numbers-- numbers eight and over-- you know, crossed them, take an eight and put it down and put another eight crosswise. And, you know, a little modification, a cut out here and have the little branch there, that's how it-- it-- it looked like a-- those little curlicues, you know, eights, eights, eights. But I didn't know the whole village. You know, when you're a kid, you're not allowed to go everywhere that you want. But the side of the-- the part of the village that was on the southwestern side, well, that I knew very well. Because we used to go. You know, we had races. Used to know who can make it faster. And we used to run all, you know, through the southwest part, all the way around the village. The other-- the-- the north-- northwest part of the village, I used to-- I got to know some of it, because the public school was on the northwest part. And the-- the Russian Orthodox Church was there. If you wanted to go to the city, you could go through. If you wanted to go to Rowne, you had to go through that part. And I used to go with my father on his excursions, you know, solicit business. My parents had a little store, a little general store. You could buy tobacco. And you could buy herring, horseshoes, horseshoe nails, axle grease for the wagons, because everybody used horse and buggies. So you had to have axle grease for the wagons-- smelly stuff. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We have sugar. We had spices, all kinds of spices. Tobacco was a big item. But used to mostly trade it, you know, barter it. You brought a couple eggs, you got maybe an ounce or two ounces of tobacco, which is a substantial amount for an egg. And the-- during the holidays, Easter time, Christmas time, New Year's, New Year's Eve, other holidays, business used to be a little bit better. Although, we had competition. I remember before the war, there was a problem. The tax collector used to be a big pain. I remember he came, once some inspector came, and he made our parents paint the walls of the store two-tone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And problem with painting, you need-- you have to have money to-- to buy oil paint. Well, it turned out there was a catastrophe, because the walls, in the store, in this particular room-- there was only a room, and maybe 8x12, 10x12-- they were all plaster, wet plaster. They were not treated for it with anything, just whitewashed. And when you put on like a oil-based, two-tone, you know, colored paint, it soaked it up like a sponge. And no matter how much you poured on it, it was never satisfied. And every time the inspector used to come, they always complained that it was not the right color or not the right shade or not enough paint on the walls. Well, I don't know what happened. But there was always a problem with it. My parents used to complain, because you had to go to the city and buy the oil the- the oil-based paint. And that's a big expense. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how much business they did. But I guess it was enough that they were able to survive. There was always a problem Friday, because you had to buy-- you know, you want to have a nice dinner for Friday night. Because the Jews welcomed the Sabbath Friday night till-- and the Sabbath lasts for 24 hours-- til Saturday night after sundown. That's when the holiday ends. Well, you have-- you would like to have, you know, a nice dinner or a nice lunch for-- a nice dinner for Friday night and a nice lunch-- nice food during the Saturday. There was always a problem, because there was not enough income to accommodate that. But we were never hungry. There was enough things to eat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I do remember, one time, my father came home in the winter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he wore a big overcoat over the-- they used to call it a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was like some kind of a-- made out of felt. And I remember, he came in. He was like a sheet of ice, one of those prehistoric men, you know, walking into the house. And my mother got up on a chair. I don't know why I was up that late. But she got up on a chair. And with a rolling pin, she hit him on the back to break the ice. Because I know now why he accumulated. Because he walked all the way from Rowne, which is-- was 14 kilometers, 10 miles, 9 miles. But that's a long distance, when you have to walk-- to walk, you know, follow the horse and-- and sleigh. All that snow accumulated on him. So that's why she was standing and hitting him with a-- with a rolling pin to get that ice off his coat. It's a [NON-ENGLISH], it's a recurring thought, [SOBBING] many times. [SOBBING] It shows you how hard, how difficult, life was over there, especially when you had no land. So you could not rely on the land. You had to always barter and trade and figure out how to-- to earn a living. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But at that time, I didn't feel bad. This was a natural thing. That's-- that's how people live. If the water is frozen-- and when you get up in the morning, and the water is frozen, well, that's too bad. You just chop the ice and start a fire on the stove. And you melt. You get on the ice, and that's how you make a cup of tea. [SIGHS] It was different. It was very different. I used to-- I, you know, when I come from work-- [SIGHS] when I come from work-- when I used to-- I'm still working now.

speaker: interviewee
text: But a few years ago, I was coming home, one winter, in my car, naturally. We had no radio in Poland. I never-- in our house, there was never a radio. Because there was no electricity, so how can you use a radio? But you could use-- could bring batteries.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was riding in the car, from work, from Livonia, Michigan, which is about 20 miles away from my house, sitting in the car, listening to the radio, a program from Boston, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold it right there, Mr. Koby.
